content.text: are the structured data objects that carry a pending action from an agent to a human reviewer. Where a

defines the trigger rules, the approval request is what actually moves through the system: it captures the action's identity, context, risk level, reviewer assignment, and current state, and it drives the notification, routing, and audit trail.
A well-designed approval request schema enables three capabilities beyond simple yes/no prompts:
(getting the request to the right reviewer quickly),
(giving reviewers enough information to make a sound decision without clicking through to other systems), and
(a full audit log of every state transition from creation to resolution).

attrs.alt: Approval request state machine (PENDING → ASSIGNED → APPROVED/DENIED/EXPIRED/CANCELLED) and four reviewer routing strategies
attrs.title: Approval Request: State Machine and Reviewer Routing

content.text: The core data model for an approval request. It is designed to be self-contained — a reviewer should be able to make a sound decision from the request alone without needing to open other systems.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import StrEnum
from typing import Any
import uuid, datetime

class ApprovalStatus(StrEnum):
    PENDING   = "pending"    # created; not yet assigned to a reviewer
    ASSIGNED  = "assigned"   # reviewer notified and working on it
    APPROVED  = "approved"   # reviewer confirmed; action will execute
    DENIED    = "denied"     # reviewer blocked; action will not execute
    EXPIRED   = "expired"    # SLA timeout; default action applied
    CANCELLED = "cancelled"  # agent or system cancelled before review

class RiskLevel(StrEnum):
    LOW      = "low"
    MEDIUM   = "medium"
    HIGH     = "high"
    CRITICAL = "critical"

@dataclass
class ApprovalRequest:
    # Identity
    request_id:      str
    session_id:      str
    agent_run_id:    str   # ties request to a specific agent invocation

    # What is being requested
    tool_name:       str
    tool_input:      dict
    action_summary:  str   # human-readable one-line description of the action
    action_detail:   str   # fuller description with context, diffs, consequences
    risk_level:      RiskLevel

    # Who should review
    required_role:   str         # e.g. "db-admin", "security-team"
    assigned_to:     str = ""    # reviewer id once assigned
    requires_n:      int = 1     # how many distinct approvals needed (two-person rule)
    approvals:       list[str] = field(default_factory=list)   # reviewer ids who approved

    # Timing
    sla_seconds:     float = 300.0
    created_at:      str = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z")
    assigned_at:     str | None = None
    resolved_at:     str | None = None
    expires_at:      str = ""    # computed from created_at + sla_seconds

    # State
    status:          ApprovalStatus = ApprovalStatus.PENDING
    resolution_note: str = ""    # reviewer's comment on decision
    deny_reason:     str = ""

    # Context for the reviewer (optional but strongly recommended)
    task_description: str = ""   # the original task the agent was given
    recent_context:   list[str] = field(default_factory=list)   # last 3-5 agent actions

    def __post_init__(self):
        if not self.expires_at:
            created = datetime.datetime.fromisoformat(self.created_at.rstrip("Z"))
            expires = created + datetime.timedelta(seconds=self.sla_seconds)
            self.expires_at = expires.isoformat() + "Z"

    @classmethod
    def create(
        cls,
        session_id:       str,
        agent_run_id:     str,
        tool_name:        str,
        tool_input:       dict,
        action_summary:   str,
        action_detail:    str,
        risk_level:       RiskLevel,
        required_role:    str,
        sla_seconds:      float = 300.0,
        task_description: str = "",
        recent_context:   list[str] | None = None,
        requires_n:       int = 1,
    ) -> "ApprovalRequest":
        return cls(
            request_id      = str(uuid.uuid4()),
            session_id      = session_id,
            agent_run_id    = agent_run_id,
            tool_name       = tool_name,
            tool_input      = tool_input,
            action_summary  = action_summary,
            action_detail   = action_detail,
            risk_level      = risk_level,
            required_role   = required_role,
            sla_seconds     = sla_seconds,
            task_description = task_description,
            recent_context  = recent_context or [],
            requires_n      = requires_n,
        )

    @property
    def is_expired(self) -> bool:
        now = datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z"
        return now > self.expires_at and self.status == ApprovalStatus.PENDING

    @property
    def approval_count(self) -> int:
        return len(set(self.approvals))

    @property
    def fully_approved(self) -> bool:
        return self.approval_count >= self.requires_n

Routing determines which human(s) receive the approval request. Four strategies cover most production needs; they can be combined (e.g., expertise-based assignment with an escalation chain as fallback):

import asyncio
from typing import Callable, Awaitable

# Reviewer registry: id -> metadata
REVIEWERS: dict[str, dict] = {
    "alice":  {"roles": ["db-admin", "ops"],     "available": True,  "tz": "America/New_York"},
    "bob":    {"roles": ["db-admin"],             "available": False, "tz": "Europe/Berlin"},
    "carol":  {"roles": ["security-team", "ops"], "available": True,  "tz": "America/Los_Angeles"},
    "dan":    {"roles": ["ops"],                  "available": True,  "tz": "Asia/Tokyo"},
}

# Role -> list of reviewer ids (precomputed from REVIEWERS)
ROLE_MAP: dict[str, list[str]] = {}
for rid, meta in REVIEWERS.items():
    for role in meta["roles"]:
        ROLE_MAP.setdefault(role, []).append(rid)

# Round-robin pointer per role
_rr_pointer: dict[str, int] = {}

def route_role_based(req: ApprovalRequest) -> list[str]:
    """Notify all reviewers with the required role; first to respond wins."""
    candidates = ROLE_MAP.get(req.required_role, [])
    available  = [r for r in candidates if REVIEWERS[r]["available"]]
    return available or candidates   # fall back to all if none available

def route_round_robin(req: ApprovalRequest) -> list[str]:
    """Assign to the next reviewer in the role pool (round-robin)."""
    candidates = ROLE_MAP.get(req.required_role, [])
    available  = [r for r in candidates if REVIEWERS[r]["available"]]
    pool       = available or candidates
    if not pool:
        return []
    idx    = _rr_pointer.get(req.required_role, 0) % len(pool)
    chosen = pool[idx]
    _rr_pointer[req.required_role] = (idx + 1) % len(pool)
    return [chosen]

# Domain -> role mapping for expertise-based routing
TOOL_DOMAIN: dict[str, str] = {
    "delete_records":  "db-admin",
    "drop_table":      "db-admin",
    "exec_shell":      "security-team",
    "deploy_service":  "ops",
    "send_email":      "ops",
}

def route_expertise_based(req: ApprovalRequest) -> list[str]:
    """Route based on the tool domain, falling back to required_role."""
    role = TOOL_DOMAIN.get(req.tool_name, req.required_role)
    return route_role_based(
        ApprovalRequest.create(
            session_id=req.session_id, agent_run_id=req.agent_run_id,
            tool_name=req.tool_name, tool_input=req.tool_input,
            action_summary=req.action_summary, action_detail=req.action_detail,
            risk_level=req.risk_level, required_role=role,
        )
    )

# Escalation chain: L1 (5 min SLA) -> L2 (15 min) -> L3 (PagerDuty)
ESCALATION_CHAIN: dict[str, list[tuple[str, float]]] = {
    "db-admin": [
        ("alice",        5 * 60),    # L1: try alice for 5 min
        ("bob",          10 * 60),   # L2: try bob for 10 more min
        ("pagerduty",    999999),    # L3: wake on-call via PagerDuty
    ],
}

async def route_with_escalation(
    req:    ApprovalRequest,
    notify: Callable[[ApprovalRequest, str], Awaitable[None]],
) -> None:
    """
    Work through the escalation chain until the request is resolved
    or the chain is exhausted.
    """
    chain = ESCALATION_CHAIN.get(req.required_role, [])
    for reviewer_id, timeout in chain:
        req.assigned_to  = reviewer_id
        req.assigned_at  = datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z"
        req.status       = ApprovalStatus.ASSIGNED
        await notify(req, reviewer_id)
        try:
            await asyncio.wait_for(
                wait_until_resolved(req), timeout=timeout
            )
            return   # resolved in this tier
        except asyncio.TimeoutError:
            continue  # escalate to next tier
    req.status = ApprovalStatus.EXPIRED

async def wait_until_resolved(req: ApprovalRequest) -> None:
    """Poll until request leaves ASSIGNED state."""
    while req.status == ApprovalStatus.ASSIGNED:
        await asyncio.sleep(1)

Each routing strategy produces a list of reviewer IDs; notifications are sent via the configured channel for each reviewer. Channel selection should respect the risk level — P1 requests warrant a phone call or PagerDuty alert, not just an email.

import json

async def notify_reviewer(req: ApprovalRequest, reviewer_id: str) -> None:
    """Dispatch a notification to the appropriate channel for the reviewer."""
    channel = choose_channel(req.risk_level)
    payload = build_notification_payload(req, reviewer_id)
    await CHANNEL_HANDLERS[channel](payload)

def choose_channel(risk: RiskLevel) -> str:
    return {
        RiskLevel.LOW:      "email",
        RiskLevel.MEDIUM:   "slack",
        RiskLevel.HIGH:     "slack_urgent",
        RiskLevel.CRITICAL: "pagerduty",
    }[risk]

def build_notification_payload(req: ApprovalRequest, reviewer_id: str) -> dict:
    return {
        "to":             reviewer_id,
        "request_id":     req.request_id,
        "action_summary": req.action_summary,
        "action_detail":  req.action_detail[:500],
        "risk_level":     req.risk_level,
        "tool_name":      req.tool_name,
        "tool_input":     req.tool_input,
        "task_context":   req.task_description[:300],
        "recent_actions": req.recent_context[-3:],
        "sla_expires":    req.expires_at,
        "approve_url":    f"/approvals/{req.request_id}/approve",
        "deny_url":       f"/approvals/{req.request_id}/deny",
    }

# Channel handlers (stubs — replace with real API calls)
async def send_email(payload: dict) -> None:
    print(f"[EMAIL] To {payload['to']}: {payload['action_summary']}")

async def send_slack(payload: dict, urgent: bool = False) -> None:
    prefix = "@channel " if urgent else ""
    print(f"[SLACK{' URGENT' if urgent else ''}] {prefix}{payload['action_summary']}")

async def page_pagerduty(payload: dict) -> None:
    print(f"[PAGERDUTY] {payload['action_summary']} — Request {payload['request_id'][:8]}")

CHANNEL_HANDLERS = {
    "email":        send_email,
    "slack":        lambda p: send_slack(p),
    "slack_urgent": lambda p: send_slack(p, urgent=True),
    "pagerduty":    page_pagerduty,
}
State Transitions and the Approval API

State transitions are the write operations on an approval request. They are the only mutations allowed — the rest of the request is immutable after creation. Each transition checks the current state to prevent double-approval, and records a timestamp.
The quality of a reviewer's decision depends on the context provided. Three fields have the most impact:
helper to generate these from the agent's current state:

In production, approval requests must survive service restarts. Use an append-only database table — PostgreSQL works well. Never update existing rows; instead insert a new row for each state transition to preserve the full audit trail.

# PostgreSQL schema (run once)
CREATE_TABLE_SQL = """
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS approval_requests (
    id           SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    request_id   UUID NOT NULL,
    event_type   TEXT NOT NULL,   -- 'created'|'assigned'|'approved'|'denied'|'expired'|'cancelled'
    session_id   UUID NOT NULL,
    agent_run_id TEXT NOT NULL,
    tool_name    TEXT NOT NULL,
    tool_input   JSONB NOT NULL,
    risk_level   TEXT NOT NULL,
    required_role TEXT NOT NULL,
    assigned_to  TEXT,
    resolution_by TEXT,
    resolution_note TEXT,
    expires_at   TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
    created_at   TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);
CREATE INDEX ON approval_requests (request_id);
CREATE INDEX ON approval_requests (session_id);
CREATE INDEX ON approval_requests (event_type, risk_level);
"""

import psycopg2, json as _json

def persist_event(
    conn,
    req:        ApprovalRequest,
    event_type: str,
    resolution_by:   str = "",
    resolution_note: str = "",
) -> None:
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute(
            """
            INSERT INTO approval_requests
              (request_id, event_type, session_id, agent_run_id, tool_name, tool_input,
               risk_level, required_role, assigned_to, resolution_by, resolution_note, expires_at)
            VALUES (%s,%s,%s,%s,%s,%s,%s,%s,%s,%s,%s,%s)
            """,
            (
                req.request_id, event_type, req.session_id, req.agent_run_id,
                req.tool_name, _json.dumps(req.tool_input),
                req.risk_level, req.required_role, req.assigned_to or None,
                resolution_by or None, resolution_note or None, req.expires_at,
            )
        )
    conn.commit()
alone is not enough for high-risk decisions. A reviewer seeing "Delete 5000 records from 'users'" needs to know which records, why, and what downstream effects to expect before clicking Approve.
No fallback when all reviewers are unavailable
: role-based routing notifies all members of a role, but if all are OOO, the request expires. Always add an escalation chain or on-call fallback for HIGH/CRITICAL requests.
, the same reviewer can approve multiple times on a two-person request. Validate uniqueness before appending.
to be updated after creation ("the agent changed what it wants to do"), the audit trail becomes meaningless. Cancel the old request and create a new one instead.
in the notification forces reviewers to click through to the full request to understand what they are approving. Include at least

An approval request is the self-contained data object that carries an agent's pending action through the review workflow. Its schema captures tool identity, structured context (summary, detail, recent agent actions, task description), risk level, reviewer assignment, SLA expiry, and state. State transitions — assigned, approved, denied, expired, cancelled — are the only mutations; all are appended to an audit log rather than updated in-place. Routing strategies (role-based, round-robin, expertise-based, escalation chain) map the
field to specific reviewers and notification channels. Rich action context — especially diffs between current and new state — is the single largest factor in approval quality.
